title: Preorder Traversal
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print its preorder traversal.
inputFormat: An integer n representing number of level-order entries
n space-separated integers with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Visit root, then recurse on left subtree, then recurse on right subtree.
wrongApproachHints: Preorder is not left-root-right.
You must stop recursion at null nodes.
hints: Base case is root == null.
Print before recursive calls.

examples.explanation: Preorder visits each node before its children.

title: Inorder Traversal
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print its inorder traversal.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Recurse on left subtree, visit root, then recurse on right subtree.
wrongApproachHints: Do not print the root before the left subtree in inorder.
hints: The root visit happens between the two recursive calls.

title: Postorder Traversal
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print its postorder traversal.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Recurse on left subtree, then right subtree, and print the root last.
wrongApproachHints: Postorder prints the root after both subtrees are complete.
hints: Base case remains root == null.
Print after both recursive calls.

examples.explanation: Postorder visits children before the parent.

title: Level Order Traversal
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print the nodes level by level from left to right.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Use a queue and repeatedly process the current front node, pushing its non-null children.
wrongApproachHints: This is a queue problem, not a recursive DFS order.
hints: Start with the root in the queue.
Pop front, print it, then push children.

title: Height of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print its height measured in number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
wrongApproachHints: Do not confuse level count with edge count unless the problem says so.
hints: Base case for null is 0.
Combine child answers with max.

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print its diameter measured in number of edges on the longest path between any two nodes.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
edgeCases: Longest path may not go through root
intendedApproachSummary: While computing subtree heights, update a global best with leftHeight + rightHeight.
wrongApproachHints: Do not recompute heights separately for every node.
hints: Return heights from recursion.
Update diameter before returning the current height.

title: Check Balanced Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print Balanced if every node has left and right subtree heights differing by at most 1; otherwise print Not Balanced.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Return subtree heights, but return a failure marker as soon as an imbalance is found.
wrongApproachHints: Checking only the root height difference is not enough.
Repeated standalone height calls make the solution O(n^2).
hints: Use -1 as an imbalance marker if you like.
Propagate imbalance upward immediately.

examples.explanation: Every node still satisfies the balance condition.

title: Search in BST
description: Given a BST in level-order form using -1 for nulls and a target value, print Found if the target exists, otherwise print Not Found.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Compare target with the current node value and move left or right accordingly.
wrongApproachHints: Do not search both subtrees blindly; use the BST property.
hints: If target < node.val, go left.
If target > node.val, go right.

examples.explanation: 5 lies in the right subtree of 4 and the left subtree of 6.

title: Left View of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print the left view of the tree.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Use level-order traversal and record the first node processed at each level.
wrongApproachHints: Do not print all nodes from every level.
The left view keeps one representative per level.
hints: Before processing a level, note the queue size.
The first node in each level is part of the answer.

examples.explanation: From the left side, these are the first visible nodes per level.

title: Insert into BST
description: Given a BST in level-order form using -1 for nulls and a value x, insert x into the BST and print its inorder traversal.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
edgeCases: Insert as leaf on either side
intendedApproachSummary: Descend left or right until a null child is found, then attach the new node there.
wrongApproachHints: Do not rebuild the whole tree from scratch.
Preserve the BST property during insertion.
hints: Compare x with current node value.
Stop when the correct child link is null.

title: Delete Node in BST
description: Given a BST in level-order form using -1 for nulls and a key, delete the key from the BST and print its inorder traversal.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
intendedApproachSummary: Handle the three delete cases carefully. For two children, replace with inorder successor or predecessor and delete that replacement node recursively.
wrongApproachHints: You cannot just drop a node with children.
Be careful not to lose subtrees when reconnecting.
hints: Find the successor in the right subtree.
Delete the successor after copying its value.

examples.explanation: 3 is replaced while preserving BST order.

title: Top View of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, print its top view from leftmost visible node to rightmost.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
edgeCases: Multiple nodes share the same horizontal distance
intendedApproachSummary: Run BFS while tracking horizontal distance. The first node seen at a distance belongs to the top view.
wrongApproachHints: DFS alone can easily take a deeper node before a shallower one.
Only the first node per horizontal distance should be kept.
hints: Store pairs of node and horizontal distance in the queue.
Use an ordered map for final left-to-right printing.

examples.explanation: These nodes are visible from the top.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of Binary Tree
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls and two node values p and q known to exist, print the value of their lowest common ancestor.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
edgeCases: One node is ancestor of the other
intendedApproachSummary: Return root if it matches p or q. Recurse into both subtrees; if both return non-null, root is the LCA.
wrongApproachHints: The first matched target is not always the final answer.
You need information from both sides.
hints: Base cases are root == null or root.val == p/q.
If both left and right answers are non-null, return root.

title: Build Tree from Preorder and Inorder
description: Given preorder and inorder traversals of a binary tree with distinct values, construct the tree and print its postorder traversal.
outputFormat: Print postorder traversal of the reconstructed tree
intendedApproachSummary: The preorder front gives the root. Use an inorder index map to split left and right subtree ranges recursively.
wrongApproachHints: Repeated linear search in inorder makes the solution too slow.
You must respect subtree boundaries while recursing.
hints: Keep a preorder index that advances as roots are created.
Map inorder values to indices for O(1) splits.

examples.explanation: That is the postorder traversal of the reconstructed tree.

title: Serialize Binary Tree (Level Order)
description: Given a binary tree in level-order form using -1 for nulls, serialize it again using level order with # for null nodes, trimming only the unnecessary trailing null markers.
inputFormat: n level-order values with -1 for null
outputFormat: Print the serialized tokens separated by spaces
edgeCases: Sparse tree needs internal null markers
Do not keep useless trailing null markers forever
intendedApproachSummary: Use a queue for BFS and emit # whenever a missing child is encountered. After traversal, trim only trailing # markers.
wrongApproachHints: Skipping null markers entirely loses the structure.
Only trailing null markers may be removed safely.
hints: Push both children of a non-null node into the queue.
Track output tokens and trim from the back at the end.

examples.explanation: The null children of node 2 must be preserved to keep the shape.
